methodology: Articles sourced from Virginia-specific RSS feeds and Google News RSS, filtered for AI/technology/workforce relevance, then assessed by Claude against the scenario framework. Each article is tagged with the region it most affects and the scenario it signals toward.

signals.title: New partnership between VCU and AI Ready RVA aims to boost AI education, innovation and community impact - VCU News
signals.signal_reading: VCU's partnership with AI Ready RVA concentrates AI education infrastructure in the state capital, potentially creating a second metro hub but also reinforcing the pattern of capability clustering around research universities rather than distributed community access.

signals.title: NVTC Spotlights Region's AI Trailblazers: Meet the 2026 AI50 - PR Newswire
signals.signal_reading: NVTC's AI50 recognition program formalizes NoVA's position as Virginia's AI center of gravity by celebrating concentrated trailblazers, signaling deepening expertise but also highlighting the geographic concentration of recognized AI leadership.

signals.title: EPA grant backs community-driven workforce training in Southwest Virginia - Virginia Tech News
signals.signal_reading: EPA-funded community-driven workforce training in Southwest Virginia represents distributed capacity-building outside traditional tech hubs, signaling potential for peer-network AI skill development in economically transitioning regions if training includes technical depth.

signals.title: NoVA School Systems Grapple with AI’s Role in the Classroom - Northern Virginia Magazine
signals.signal_reading: School systems grappling with AI's classroom role signals shallow, platform-delivered adoption spreading through education infrastructure—widespread access but likely limited to consumer-grade tools rather than developing technical AI fluency among students.

signals.title: AI is shaping health care. Maryland and Virginia are regulating it very differently - WAMU
signals.signal_reading: Virginia's divergent healthcare AI regulatory approach from Maryland indicates state-level policy fragmentation that may either concentrate innovation in permissive jurisdictions or create uneven AI capability distribution across healthcare systems statewide.
